_comment: The procedure code SN031A maps to HBP 1.0 codes S800005 and S800006, all covering Excision of Cervical Ribs.
clinical_indications: Localized pain, compression of surrounding structures requiring intervention

Nerve compression causing symptoms like diminished distal pulse, prolongation of capillary refill, discoloration, decreased temperature of the distal upper extremity, or gangrenous changes at fingertips
Neurological manifestations due to cervical rib

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure, and planned line of treatment?
Did the Cervical X-ray/CT/MRI Chest and Cervical Spine confirm the diagnosis?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?
Was the Chest/Cervical X-ray post-surgery report submitted?

common_queries: Clinical presentation or imaging not indicative of surgery.
Clinical notes (detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure, or planned line of treatment) missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Cervical X-ray/CT/MRI Chest and Cervical Spine not confirming diagnosis at pre-authorization.
Detailed Procedure / Operative Notes missing at claim submission.
Post-surgery Chest/Cervical X-ray report not submitted at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: A cervical rib, also known as a 'neck rib' or 'supernumerary rib in the cervical region,' is a congenital overdevelopment of the transverse process of a cervical spine vertebra.
They are commonly attached to the seventh cervical vertebra. They vary in size, shape, attachment sites, and can occur unilaterally or bilaterally.
The cervical rib typically consists of a head, neck, and tubercle. It is attached posteriorly to the first rib by a fibrous band near in the insertion of the anterior scalene muscle.
Cervical ribs must articulate with the transverse process to be considered a rib. Unilateral cervical ribs are more common and found more frequently on the left side than the right side.

There are four types of cervical ribs: Type 1 (complete rib articulating with first rib or manubrium), Type 2 (incomplete rib with a free distal tip), Type 3 (incomplete rib with a distal fibrous band attachment), Type 4 (short piece of bone extending beyond the C7 transverse process).
surgical_options: Complete resection of the rib is the mainstay of therapy; surgical approach varies.
Supraclavicular and transaxillary approaches with or without a first rib excision or scalenectomy.
A posterior approach may also be an option for specific indications.
The supraclavicular approach is primarily preferred for arterial compression.
The transaxillary approach is the most common approach, reserved for subclavian/axillary venous occlusion or neurological compression.
The posterior approach is the choice during re-operation for decompression of the brachial plexus and vascular structures.
